comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the use of Starlink satellite internet on flights, with users sharing experiences, opinions on its impact, and technical questions. Some highlight the benefits of free or fast inflight internet, while others express concerns about cost, privacy, and social implications.

comments.author: raw_anon_1111
comments.text: Starlink is good I’m sure. But it isn’t the be all end all of high speed internet on planes.  Delta doesn’t use Starlink and most of its planes have fast satellite internet except the A900s used for short hops.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and accurate perspective on Starlink's role in inflight internet, noting that other providers also offer competitive services.

comments.author: Hansenq
comments.text:
Ben Thompson interviewed UA's CEO on Starlink a few months ago.

Scott said: "It took time to negotiate, because we wanted to own the consumer data, and at the beginning, Starlink did, so that was hard, and then, the other thing was I wanted to let my big competitors in the United States finish their deals with other providers and get locked in so that we would — eventually, everyone’s going to have Starlink."

Brilliant. Just brilliant. Ensured that UA would be first (of the 3 major US carriers) to Starlink and that everyone else had to wait until their existing agreements multi-year expired before switching. UA's best CEO in decades!

https://stratechery.com/2026/an-interview-with-united-ceo-sc...
comments.analysis: This comment shares insider information from a credible interview, explaining strategic business decisions around Starlink adoption by United Airlines.

comments.author: rayiner
comments.text: I tried Starlink on a United flight the other day (short hop from Hilton Head to DC) and it was amazing.
comments.analysis: A firsthand user experience praising Starlink's performance on a flight, providing anecdotal evidence of its effectiveness.

comments.author: apitman
comments.text: I've only had it once, but inflight Starlink is a game changer. I was able to play a ranked AoE2 game over the Pacific Ocean.
comments.analysis: Another personal account emphasizing the low latency and reliability of Starlink inflight, though anecdotal and limited in scope.

comments.author: dvno42
comments.text: United has this on some flights. It's no cost but they force you watch ads in the captive portal. I'd rather pay the $8 and be left in peace, every time.
comments.analysis: Provides insight into the user experience with Starlink inflight internet, highlighting trade-offs between free access with ads versus paid ad-free service.

comments.author: devin
comments.text: I've written it elsewhere, but: it is such a shame that the United States saw fit to run electricity _everywhere_, no matter how rural your location, but instead of do the same for rural internet we had to wait for... a private company to launch a global network of satellites. Yes, this post is about internet access while traveling 500mph, which is a different problem, but it is so messed up that people fall over themselves about Starlink for rural connectivity when it is an incredibly complex and expensive technology with huge ongoing costs that could have been solved once and for all by simply running some wires.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid societal critique about infrastructure priorities, though somewhat tangential to the main topic of inflight Starlink.

comments.author: 6thbit
comments.text:
Why does it work on the plane? are the constant handoffs between satellites not enough to break connections or cause extremely high packet loss for it to suck?

is there a speed at which it would break?
comments.analysis: Asks relevant technical questions about Starlink's performance at high speeds and satellite handoffs, indicating curiosity and understanding of network challenges.

comments.author: gpt5
comments.text: One nice thing about Starlink is that they force the airlines to offer it for free. I’m not sure why SpaceX is doing this, but it was surprising enough to me that my international WiFi was not only fast, but completely free that I researched it.
comments.analysis: This claim about Starlink forcing airlines to offer free internet is questionable and lacks supporting evidence; likely a misunderstanding or exaggeration.

comments.author: torcete
comments.text: People are so rude with their phones that I fear that starlink becomes popular in all flights.
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective social concern unrelated to the technical or factual discussion about Starlink's capabilities.

comments.author: throwaway132448
comments.text: No internet on flights is one of my favourite features.
comments.analysis: A personal preference statement that does not contribute to the analysis or understanding of Starlink or inflight internet.
